content.full_text:
Jeanne Mahoney
32603 Kenita Way
Union City, CA 94587
February 9, 1990


The Fair Political Practices Commission
428 J Street
Sacramento, CA 95814

RE: Conflict of interest for Lynne Mattos as Planning
    Commissioner for the City of Union City

Dear Commissioners:

     The majority of the City Council of the City of Union City
has appointed Lynne Mattos, a Crime Prevention Specialist
employed by the City of Union City, to the Planning Commission.
Councilmember Dick Oliver has raised some grave objections to
this appointment, citing two separate grounds for conflict of
interest for Lynne Mattos if she serves the City as a paid
employee, and fills a position which requires impartiality.

     The issue here is not whether Lynne Mattos is dedicated to
the community, nor is it a question of her great knowledge of the
community. The issue is one of conflict of interest. The
questions that must be answered are these: Are applicants
well-served, is the public well-served, by a commissioner whose
perspective is that of a paid staff member? Can any applicant,
can the public be sure that any decision made by Lynne Mattos is
unbiased? The answer is no.

     The media have reported that the FPPC finds no "direct
conflict". The conflict is continual, it is inherent!

     Conflict will exist for Lynne Mattos in two ways:

1. All development affects the City budget, and therefore the
Police Department Budget. It is not reasonable to believe that,
as a Police Department employee, Lynne Mattos can be impartial
when making decisions which will directly affect the budget of
her own department, and therefore her own salary and her own
work-load, and those of her fellow officers.

2. The Police Department participates in a closed-door Design
Review Process, which dramatically shapes the product which comes
before the Planning Commission. The potential for conflict exists
in each and every plan that is reviewed. Lynne Mattos has access
to information, and an opportunity for input that is not
available to other Planning Commissioners, or even applicants
themselves.


[illegible] [illegible] L [illegible]

     [illegible]

Page Two

     Further, if the Police Department has objections to a plan,
it is possible that an applicant's request for review and
reconsideration would not receive an impartial review from a
Planning Commissioner whose salary comes from that same Police
Department.

     A staff employee brings the perspective of staff.  The
purpose of a Citizen's Review Board (the Planning Commission) is
to allow for the perspective of an impartial and unbiased
citizen. The staff already has an opportunity to have input on
plans, it is wrong to allow a staff person to fill a seat that
belongs to a citizen not already involved in the formal process.
Applicants who must participate in the process deserve better
than this.

     This matter is of so much importance for the well-being of
our City, that I am requesting that the FPPC seriously examine
the definition of conflict, and re-think their position. If the
FPPC is incapable of seeing the blatant conflict which exists,
they fail to protect the public which they are sworn to serve.
The public looks to the FPPC to establish and maintain the
highest standards of fairness.

     The Mayor, Tom Kitayama, and Councilmembers Alice Arce and
Manny Fernandez failed the citizens of Union City because they
failed to ensure impartiality in selecting a staff member. We
must look to the FPPC, and hope they exercise better judgment.

     Thank you for your consideration of this matter.

     Yours sincerely,

     Jeanne Mahoney

cc: The Editor, The Argus Newspapers
    The Editor, The San Jose Mercury News
